learning: Homebrew Python 3.14 pyexpat can break pre-commit remote Python hook environment creation before repo hooks run; local language: system hooks avoided that failing layer while preserving checks.

summary: Bootstrap chrome plugins run in a different JS scope than the test env. Bugs like 'Node is not defined' (chrome scope lacks DOM globals like Node) and Xray-wrapper underscore-property hiding (event.reader._iframe) only manifest in real Zotero, not test env. The e2e harness's own use of _dispatchEvent bypassed Zotero's customEvent bridge, so the harness saw 'fix works' while real Zotero saw bug. Mitigation: 3-tier fallback for chrome lookups (wrappedJSObject -> direct -> DOM-search) AND switching harness to dispatch via real customEvent bridge.

mcp__codex__codex is callable from the orchestrator session but DENIED from subagent contexts (even with sandbox:read-only). Forge code-review iterations must be driven directly from the orchestrator, not delegated to a 'codex-tests' subagent. Costs context but unavoidable.

BUG-AC8-1 class: provider adapters that read happy-path body fields and yield message_end on stream end mask upstream errors (HTTP 4xx/5xx with error JSON). Fix is to detect terminal error chunks (top-level error key OR done_reason==='error') and yield in-band error event. Recurred at proxy layer until iter-2 codex review caught it again.

new URL('/api/tags', baseUrl) STRIPS path prefixes (e.g. localhost:11400/codex becomes localhost:11400/api/tags), causing the wrong endpoint to be probed. Always concatenate: trimmedBase + '/api/tags'. Caught when user combined /codex URL with gemma model and validator wrongly accepted.

Parallel agent dispatch (~3-5 at once) is highly productive but requires explicit file-scope partitioning in each agent's brief. Without it, agents step on bootstrap.ts and settings-view.ts. Pattern: 'orchestrator does the final integration; subagents export wireX() functions that orchestrator wires in bootstrap.'

Settings dialog rendered inputs for an entire session before anyone noticed: NO event handlers were ever attached. Users typed values that vanished on dialog close. Caught only by user manual smoke. Lesson: render+wire pattern needed an integration test that proves Save persists to prefs and reopen reads them back.

Codex code review caught 4 ACs that passed unit tests but did not deliver end-to-end: crawler supported Zotero.PDFWorker but bootstrap never threaded it; selection.source was populated but never put in the LLM prompt; openCitationInReader ignored the resolved attachmentKey. Unit tests with injected deps mask missing production wiring. Verify the bootstrap/runtime actually threads each dependency, not just that the consumer accepts it.

The plan HYPOTHESIZED the v0.2.0 e2e failures were an auto-reindex notifier race and mandated /rca-before-fix. /rca REFUTED it: real cause was the e2e driver using a global document.querySelector that hit a stale onboarding dialog, returning a detached button. Plan hypotheses about bug causation must be framed as hypotheses and verified by /rca before any fix is written.

The spec said '5 failing e2e tests since v0.2.0'; the actual CI run had 6 (AC3-scroll was missed). When scoping a 'fix the failing tests' AC, enumerate failures from the actual CI run log, not from a prose summary — the missed one (AC3-scroll) would have kept the release gate red.

rag-augmented-provider re-read + JSON.parse-d the entire 71MB per-provider index file on EVERY chat request (no cache), then ran cosine over all chunks — a multi-second tax per turn that surfaced only when the user reported 25s latency. Fixed with a memoized IndexStorage.read() keyed on a (meta-sidecar, primary stat) fingerprint. A size-only stat is not a sound change token — must include mtime or disable caching.

When a CLI's documented one-shot subcommand (codex exec --json) doesn't stream, sibling subcommands may speak a different protocol that does. codex exec --json emits only item.completed at end; codex mcp-server emits per-token agent_message_content_delta over JSON-RPC at ~20ms intervals — same binary, drop-in per-turn lifecycle. Always smoke-test every subcommand surface before declaring a CLI non-streaming; protocol surface ≠ CLI surface. Investigation effort: 4 Proposer rounds (exec flags + env + PTY + features → exec-server/review/remote-control → app-server per-turn → mcp-server).

Proposer round 2 rejected codex app-server with 'requires long-lived daemon lifecycle' — an UNVERIFIED assumption. User challenge prompted empirical test: app-server runs cleanly per-turn (initialize → thread/start → turn/start → deltas → exit on stdin close, ~20ms). The 'daemon' framing was a marketing hangover from how OpenAI ships it for their desktop app, not a CLI requirement. Apply: when a Proposer says 'X requires Y', the result record MUST cite evidence (a smoke run, a doc quote with link). 'It looks like Y' is not evidence.

codex mcp-server spawned with default config inherits user-configured ~/.codex/config.toml [mcp_servers] block — spawns ALL configured MCP sidecars (XcodeBuildMCP, etc.) per turn, adding 1-3s startup latency. Suppress with argv -c 'mcp_servers={}' (empty TOML inline table). The efficiency review caught this; without it, the streaming patch's latency goal would be silently undermined for any user with MCP servers configured.

When the proxy spawns user-installed CLIs (codex, claude), the CLI inherits the user's personal dev config — global CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, skills (Superpowers, logseq-memory), MCP plugins. Users see preambles like 'I'll load the Superpowers instruction first' before every PDF-explain answer. Fix requires environment-based isolation: codex needs HOME+CODEX_HOME+cwd at a clean tmpdir (plus copying auth.json into it; `codex mcp-server` does NOT expose --ignore-user-config); claude needs cwd+`--setting-sources user --strict-mcp-config --disable-slash-commands --system-prompt <NEUTRAL>` (NOT --bare, which breaks subscription OAuth). Accepted residual: 5 bundled codex .system skills + claude SessionStart hooks both bypass these defenses; --system-prompt is the bias against them.

When a feature ships behind a single surface (library-chat citation linkification, wired only in library-chat-view), reviewing 'is the feature done?' must include 'do peer surfaces need it too?' The plugin had three surfaces consuming the same RAG provider (popup, sidebar, library-chat) but the click-handler + tokenizing renderer was wired only in library-chat. Users noticed by reporting plain-text [DPZZ8V4Q] in the popup. Apply: when reviewing a feature that touches one surface, grep for sibling surfaces and call out the asymmetry as a follow-up if not in scope.

PopupRetrievalChannel subscribers leaked through the 'popup → continue to sidebar → dismiss sidebar' path: popup transitioned to sidebar programmatically (no onDismiss fire), sidebar's onDismiss only tore down sidebarUnsubscribe (not retrievalUnsubscribe). Each cycle leaked one permanent subscriber, channel publish became O(leaked-subscribers). Caught by efficiency review at /simplify, not by tests. Fix: idempotent tearDownRetrieval owner called from every teardown path (popup dismiss, sidebar dismiss, cleanupExplain, programmatic transitions). Apply: any 'transfer ownership between two UI surfaces' path needs a single-owner teardown helper, not per-surface unsubscribers.

A blanket bearer-token gate on a local proxy quietly breaks the orphan-takeover path: when a new plugin instance starts after Zotero restart, the orphan proxy on the same port has a DIFFERENT random token (the old plugin instance is gone). The new instance can't authenticate to POST /api/shutdown to evict it, so spawn fails. Fix: carve /api/shutdown (and /, the route listing) out of bearer auth while keeping Host+Origin gates. DoS via shutdown is acceptable on a single-user 127.0.0.1-bound service; loss of orphan-takeover is not. Apply: any process-lifecycle endpoint that crosses generations of the parent process needs to work without parent-generation-specific credentials.

When a parent process sets an env var and a child process reads it, typo'd literals on either side silently break the contract. The proxy reads `process.env.LLM_PROXY_AUTH_TOKEN`; the plugin sets `LLM_PROXY_AUTH_TOKEN` on the subprocess env. A typo on one side would silently disable auth in production (the no-auth-mode permit-with-warn fallback would activate, masking the bug). Fix: hoist the env-var name to a single shared constant module (`scripts/llm-proxy/protocol-constants.mjs`) imported by both sides, plus a round-trip test that asserts parent-set-token === proxy-read-token via the same constant on both sides. Apply: any string identifier crossing a process boundary needs to be a single-source constant + a round-trip assertion.

For security defaults on opt-out paths (the no-auth dev fallback for `npm run proxy:llm`), permit-with-canary is more robust than hard-fail. Hard-fail breaks dev tooling and existing integration tests; permit-with-canary keeps them working AND emits a loud `console.warn('llm-proxy: AUTH DISABLED — set LLM_PROXY_AUTH_TOKEN to enable bearer auth')` at startup so a misconfigured production spawn shouts in the Zotero error console (the canary triggers the user's attention). The plugin-side test asserts the env var IS set, so a wiring regression is also caught by unit test. Two layers: canary at runtime + assertion at test time. Apply: when a security default has legitimate opt-out cases, prefer this two-layer pattern over hard-fail.

`requestBaseUrl.startsWith('http://127.0.0.1:<port>')` is too strict for proxy auth gating because the plugin's own presets use `localhost:<port>` (a different string but the same address). The proxy server's allowed-hosts Set correctly accepted both forms; the plugin-side closure didn't. Result: every Codex Proxy + Claude Proxy popup chat 401'd in production with 1095/1095 tests passing because no test exercised the preset URL combination. Fix: URL-parse the request URL and compare `hostname ∈ {127.0.0.1, localhost} AND port === proxyPort`. Apply: any 'is this URL ours?' check must be host-normalized (URL-parsed), never raw-string-prefix-matched, especially when the two systems (parent vs child / plugin vs proxy) both implement their own version of the check.

Same pattern as the 2026-05-22 pdf-context-features learning: a unit-tested capability (the bootstrap.ts getProxyAuthHeader closure) was wired into ONE call site (ollama adapter chat/embed) but not into THREE others (model-discovery, runtime probe, onboarding probe). Tests passed because each test injected its own deps; production failed because the unwired sites just sent no header. Catch: after adding a new cross-cutting dep, grep for `fetch(` against the same target and confirm every site receives the dep. The 2 reactive symptoms (popup 401 + settings model-discovery 401) tipped us off — but the third (probeOneUrl validation 401) would have surfaced only when the user clicked Save and the fourth (onboarding) only on first-run. Lesson: when shipping a new gate, audit ALL the call sites the gate guards in the same PR.

The 2026-05-24 per-route-auth-carve-out-for-lifecycle learning got the SHUTDOWN endpoint right (bearer-exempt so cross-generation takeovers work) but missed that the OWNERSHIP-DETECTION probe ALSO crosses generations. The takeover code in wire-proxy-lifecycle.ts called bearer-gated /api/diagnostics with the CURRENT generation's token to check 'is this orphan ours?'; the orphan (from a prior generation) 401'd → 'not ours' → no shutdown attempted → EADDRINUSE → 'Proxy exited'. The shutdown carve-out was correct but unreachable because gated behind a bearer-required probe. Fix: probe the bearer-exempt GET / route (returns {name: 'zotero-ai-llm-proxy', routes: [...]}, our signature) for ownership, then POST the bearer-exempt /api/shutdown. Apply: when adding a security gate to a system that has multi-generational state (orphans, prior-version clients, etc.), enumerate EVERY probe + action chain a future generation might run and verify each step works with NO shared secret from the prior generation.

When a parent process spawns a child via Subprocess/child_process and runs `drainStderr` as fire-and-forget (`void async () => { await reader.readString() ... }`), a fast-crashing child loses its stderr in the UI: `wait()` resolves before `readString()` consumes any chunks, so `stderrBuffer` is empty at snapshot time. Pattern filed at logseq/Claude___Patterns___stderr-exit-race-loses-error-message. Fix: return a Promise from drainStderr, store as `drainerDone`, await `Promise.race([drainerDone, sleep(graceMs=500)])` in the exit handler before snapshotting. Apply: any subprocess UI that surfaces stderr-on-exit MUST gate the snapshot on a drainer-complete signal, not on `wait()` resolution alone.

Zotero/Firefox auto-update via manifest update_url silently replaces a locally-installed XPI with the GitHub-release artifact when both share the same version number (`update_hash` mismatch path on top of standard newer-wins logic). Developers testing branch builds see their install clobbered back to the released version within minutes — making local testing of unreleased changes impossible until the lockout. Fix: bump the dev-branch manifest+package version to `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH-dev` so Zotero treats local installs as strictly newer than the release and never auto-updates. Keep `parseReleaseVersion`'s regex STRICT (rejects `-dev`) so accidental release tags fail loudly; gate the local-build path in package-xpi.mjs on absent tag arg instead. Apply: any plugin distributed via update_url AND built locally for testing needs a dev-suffix convention OR a separate update-channel; same-version content mismatches are clobbered by default.

macOS has no equivalent to Linux's PR_SET_PDEATHSIG, so a child process orphans to launchd when the parent dies. The cross-platform POSIX workaround: when the parent's stdin pipe closes (which happens automatically when the parent exits if the child was spawned with stdio:'pipe'), the child detects EOF and self-terminates. Three gotchas filed: (1) `process.stdin.resume()` is required — Node default-pauses stdin so 'end' events don't fire without resume; (2) the shutdown handler must be idempotent (concurrent EOF + SIGTERM both fire) — use a single-shot guard; (3) any callsite spawning the proxy with `stdio: 'ignore'` for stdin will trigger immediate startup self-shutdown — tests that don't want this must use `stdio: 'pipe'` and hold the parent end open. Pattern applies to any subprocess that should not survive its parent on POSIX, regardless of whether the parent dies cleanly or via SIGKILL.

Codex external review (sandbox: read-only) of a cross-platform Node service surfaced 5 Ubuntu blockers that local development on macOS had missed: (1) Mozilla Subprocess.sys.mjs stdin auto-pipe behavior is verified on macOS but unspecified on Linux — explicit `stdin: "pipe"` is required to make the new stdin-EOF detector survive on Linux. (2) Ubuntu apt's /usr/bin/node may be Node 12/14 (no globalThis.fetch); the auto-detect prefers /usr/bin/node so users on outdated apt Node hit a fetch-undefined crash unless candidate-walk validates the version. (3) PATH discovery fallback omits /snap/bin, /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin, ~/.linuxbrew/bin — Linux-common locations. (4) Per-user Linuxbrew candidate missing from home-relative shims. (5) `${shell} -lc` rejected by dash (Ubuntu's /bin/sh); branch by shell basename: `-c` for sh/dash, `-lc` for bash/zsh/ksh. Apply: any cross-platform spawn path needs a Linux-specific code review even when developed/tested on macOS; codex MCP `read-only` is a cheap way to get one.

Production Mozilla chrome JS (the runtime Zotero uses for plugins) cannot `import "node:child_process"` — those modules exist only in Node. To run a synchronous subprocess from chrome scope, use Mozilla's Subprocess.sys.mjs (`Subprocess.call({...})` + `proc.wait()` is async; for sync collect-output use the same with await). A unit-test path that imports `node:child_process` works in vitest but is a no-op in production: the dep defaults must fail-open (skip validation) when the host can't supply the subprocess primitive. The Node-version validator landed as fail-open with a follow-up TODO to wire chrome's Subprocess.call as the production spawnSync replacement.
